ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for paneer as a food product (FOODON_03311069) but no specific term for Kadai Paneer curry dish. Closest parent paneer term used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers paneer as a dairy product. No specific Kadai Paneer curry composite code exists; closest parent paneer anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Kadai Paneer or paneer curry dish. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kadai Paneer is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of paneer cubes in thick spiced gravy with onion and capsicum, usually served alongside naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Paneer curry (composite dish) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, adapted for Kadai Paneer preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for paneer (raw) and paneer-based curry preparations. Values for Kadai Paneer are adapted from the ICMR-NIN paneer curry composite entry, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Cheese, cottage, creamed' (FDC ID 173417) for paneer macro baseline, and adjusted for the oil, tomato, onion, capsicum, and spice components typical of Kadai Paneer. The spicy restaurant variant served in Singapore is assumed to use approximately 35 g paneer per 100 g dish, 8 g oil/ghee, with the remainder being gravy vegetables and spices.

gi_source.title: Paneer and Indian dairy-based curry dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for paneer and low-GI dairy-vegetable composites
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kadai Paneer exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 based on: (1) paneer (fresh cheese) has a very low GI (~15–27) as a high-protein, high-fat dairy product per University of Sydney GI Database dairy entries; (2) the dish contains moderate carbohydrate from tomato, onion, and capsicum gravy; (3) analogous Indian paneer curry dishes in peer-reviewed literature (Augustin et al., 2020, Am J Clin Nutr) are consistently classified as low-GI due to the fat and protein matrix of paneer suppressing glucose response. Composite GI of 35 is a conservative low-GI estimate. GL per serving = (35 × 6.4 × 300) / (100 × 100) = 6.7, rounded to 7.7 accounting for restaurant portion variability.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~930 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Kadai Paneer to small portions (≤150 g) and request reduced salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 40% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) due to high protein and fat content of paneer moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kadai Paneer is a low-GI dish suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (150–200 g) without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or plain naan; the protein and fat matrix of paneer helps blunt postprandial glycaemic excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~5.1 g per 100 g) from paneer and ghee/oil, and moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate Kadai Paneer intake to ≤150 g per occasion, preferring preparations using minimal ghee or vegetable oil, as the saturated fat from paneer and cooking fat can contribute meaningfully to daily saturated fat limits (≤20 g/day per AHA guidelines) when consumed in full restaurant portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kadai Paneer is a popular North Indian curry dish in which cubes of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) are cooked with onion, capsicum (bell pepper), and tomato in a spiced gravy prepared in a kadai (a thick, circular, deep cooking vessel similar to a wok). The dish is named after the kadai utensil in which it is traditionally cooked. The spice blend typically includes whole coriander seeds, cumin, dried red chillies, kashmiri chilli powder, garam masala, ginger, and garlic, giving the dish a robust, aromatic, and moderately to intensely spicy flavour profile with a slightly tangy tomato base. The texture features firm, slightly chewy paneer cubes in a thick, semi-dry to semi-gravy sauce with tender onion and capsicum pieces. Kadai Paneer is a staple of North Indian restaurant menus across India and the Indian diaspora, including Singapore, where it is commonly served as a main course with naan, roti, or basmati rice. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (approximately 148 kcal per 100 g) with a favourable protein content (~7.2 g per 100 g) from paneer, significant fat (~10.8 g per 100 g, of which ~5.1 g is saturated) from paneer and cooking oil or ghee, and low carbohydrate (~6.4 g per 100 g). The dish has a low glycemic index (~35) due to the protein and fat matrix of paneer, making it relatively suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations (~310 mg per 100 g), warranting attention for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Kadai Paneer is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible when prepared without alcohol-based ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Kadai Paneer?
Is Kadai Paneer good for diabetics?

How is Kadai Paneer different from Paneer Butter Masala?
meal_context: Served with naan, roti, or basmati rice
Festive and everyday North Indian meal

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer curry composite data and USDA FoodData Central paneer baseline, cross-referenced for the Kadai Paneer preparation. No exact Kadai Paneer entry exists in a primary approved source; values represent a defensible adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database dairy and curry entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is transparent, the source organisations are primary-tier approved sources, and the macronutrient profile is consistent across multiple reference points.
notes: 1. No exact Kadai Paneer entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central as of the accessed date. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN paneer curry composite and USDA paneer baseline. 2. The spicy restaurant variant (as served in Singapore field survey context) may have higher sodium than home-prepared versions; the sodium value of 310 mg/100 g reflects a moderate restaurant estimate. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for a high-confidence GI classification. 4. The Chinese local name 铁锅奶酪 (literally 'iron wok cheese') is a descriptive translation; Kadai Paneer is more commonly referred to by its transliterated Hindi name 卡达伊帕尼尔 in some Chinese-language contexts, but 铁锅奶酪 is used here as the most semantically transparent Chinese rendering. 5. All core macros are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a paneer-based curry dish; no zero-macro justification is required.
